revision.comment: 南木神社について
revision.text: 大楠公を祀っている。楠木正成を祀る最古の神社。後村上天皇から南木（なぎ）明神の神号を賜わった。 元禄１０年（１９６７）、社殿が傾いたため、現在地に移転した。しかし、移転した社殿も昭和９年に暴風雨により、 (室戸台風により）老松が倒れ、崩壊した。〔大楠公を祀ったご神体は厨子に守られ無傷だった〕 現社殿は昭和１５年に、再建されたものである。

revision.comment: Categoryなど
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 南木神社 |画像 = <!--[[ファイル:|250px]]--> |所在地 = [[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]水分357 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|40|53|N|135|50|54|E|type:landmark_region:JP-29|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = |神体 = |社格 = |創建 = |札所等 = |例祭 = |本殿 = }} '''南木神社'''（なぎじんじゃ）は、[[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]にある楠木正成を祀る最古の神社。[[建水分神社]]の[[摂社]]である。[[後村上天皇]]から南木（なぎ）明神の神号を賜わった。
== 歴史 == 元禄10年（1967年）、社殿が傾いたため、現在地に移転した。しかし、移転した社殿も昭和9年に暴風雨により、(室戸台風により）老松が倒れ、崩壊した。〔大楠公を祀ったご神体は厨子に守られ無傷だった〕
現社殿は昭和15年に、再建されたものである。
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[建水分神社]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ 建水分神社] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なきしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の歴史]]

{{出典の明記|date=2015年12月29日 (火) 14:13 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称= 南木神社 |画像= {{画像募集中|cat=南河内郡}} |所在地= [[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]水分357 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 36 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N |経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 37 | 経度秒 = 53 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |地図国コード = JP-29 |地図= Japan Osaka |祭神= |神体= |社格= |創建= |札所等= |例祭= |本殿= }} '''南木神社'''（なぎじんじゃ）は、[[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]にある、[[楠木正成]]を祀る最古の神社。[[建水分神社]]の[[摂社]]である。[[後村上天皇]]から南木（なぎ）明神の神号を賜わった。
== 歴史 == 元禄10年（1697年）、社殿が傾いたため、現在地に移転した。しかし、移転した社殿も昭和9年（1934年）に暴風雨（[[室戸台風]]）により老松が倒れ、崩壊した〔大楠公を祀ったご神体は厨子に守られ無傷だった〕。
現社殿は昭和15年（1940年）に再建されたものである。
== 出典 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[建水分神社]]

{{出典の明記|date=2015年12月29日 (火) 14:13 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称= 南木神社 |画像= [[File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|300px]]
楠木正成木像（南木神社所蔵） |所在地= [[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]水分357 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 36 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N |経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 37 | 経度秒 = 53 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |地図国コード = JP-29 |地図= Japan Osaka |祭神= |神体= |社格= |創建= |札所等= |例祭= |本殿= }} '''南木神社'''（なぎじんじゃ）は、[[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]にある、[[楠木正成]]を祀る最古の神社。[[建水分神社]]の[[摂社]]である。[[後村上天皇]]から南木（なぎ）明神の神号を賜わった。 == 歴史 == 元禄10年（1697年）、社殿が傾いたため、現在地に移転した。しかし、移転した社殿も昭和9年（1934年）に暴風雨（[[室戸台風]]）により老松が倒れ、崩壊した〔大楠公を祀ったご神体は厨子に守られ無傷だった〕。 現社殿は昭和15年（1940年）に再建されたものである。 == 出典 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[建水分神社]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ 建水分神社] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なきしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の建築物]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の歴史]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:楠木氏|社]] [[Category:楠木正成]]

{{神社 |名称= 南木神社 |画像= [[File:Nagijinjya view.jpg|260px]]
拝殿と鳥居（2020年11月） |所在地= [[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]水分357 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 36 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N |経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 37 | 経度秒 = 53 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |地図国コード = JP-29 |地図= Japan Osaka |祭神= 大楠公 ([[楠木正成]]) |神体= |社格= |創建= |札所等= |例祭= |本殿= }} '''南木神社'''（なぎじんじゃ）は、[[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]にある、[[楠木正成]]を祀る最古の神社。[[建水分神社]]の[[摂社]]である。[[後村上天皇]]から南木（なぎ）明神の神号を賜わった。 == 歴史 == 元禄10年（1697年）、社殿が傾いたため、現在地に移転した。しかし、移転した社殿も[[昭和]]9年（1934年）に暴風雨（[[室戸台風]]）により老松が倒れ、崩壊した〔大楠公を祀ったご神体は厨子に守られ無傷だった〕。 == 文化財 == [[2020年]] ([[令和]]2年) 7月17日、以下が[[文化庁]]から[[登録有形文化財]]に答申された{{citeweb|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642 |publisher=大阪府|title=国登録有形文化財（建造物）の登録について|accessdate=2020-11-21}}{{citeweb|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf |publisher=大阪府|title=登録文化財の詳細説明 |accessdate=2020-11-21}}。 ===本殿=== *昭和13年 ([[1938年]]） から15年 ([[1940年]]) にかけて再建、[[平成]]16年 ([[2004年]])・同29年 ([[2017年]]) 改修、一[[間]]社[[春日造]]の社殿。 ===拝殿及び幣殿=== *昭和15年建築、平成16年改修で、本殿前に建ち、[[向拝]]の中央間を一段高くし 向唐[[破風]]屋根を付け、背面には唐破風屋根の幣殿が接続する特異な形式。 ===築地塀=== *昭和13年建築。[[参道]]の入口付近に位置する宝庫とともに、[[社叢]]の[[景観]]を形成するものと評価された。 他、楠木正成木像を所有する。 Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|本殿 Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|拝殿及び幣殿 Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|楠木正成木像 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[登録有形文化財一覧]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ 建水分神社] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なきしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:大阪府の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の建築物]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の歴史]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:楠木氏|社]] [[Category:楠木正成]]

revision.comment: 校正をしました。
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 南木神社 |画像= [[File:Nagijinjya view.jpg|260px]]
拝殿と鳥居（2020年11月） |所在地= [[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]水分357 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 36 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N |経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 37 | 経度秒 = 53 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |地図国コード = JP-29 |地図= Japan Osaka |祭神= 大楠公 ([[楠木正成]]) |神体= |社格= |創建= [[延元]]2年/[[建武 (日本)|建武]]4年（[[1337年]]） |札所等= |例祭= |本殿= }} '''南木神社'''（なぎじんじゃ）は、[[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]水分にある[[神社]]。[[建水分神社]]の[[摂社]]であり、[[楠木正成]]を祀る最古の神社である。 == 歴史 == [[後醍醐天皇]]の臣・[[楠木正成]]が[[延元]]元年/[[建武 (日本)|建武]]3年（[[1336年]]）5月25日に[[湊川の戦い]]で[[足利尊氏]]に敗れ、自害した。その翌年の延元2年/建武4年（[[1337年]]）に、[[楠木氏]]の[[氏神]]である[[建水分神社]]の境内に正成を祭神とする神社を建てることとなり、[[後村上天皇]]から「楠木」の名字をもじった南木（なぎ）明神という神号を賜わり、南木神社が建水分神社の本殿の背後に建立された。 [[江戸時代]]にはこの近辺は[[伊勢国]][[神戸藩]][[石川氏]]の領地となっていた。そのため、[[元禄]]10年（[[1697年]]）に本殿が傾いた際は藩主・[[石川総茂]]の命により現在地に移転している。 [[1934年]]（[[昭和]]9年）の[[室戸台風]]により近くの老松が折れてしまい本殿は倒壊した。しかし、御神体である大楠公（楠木正成）尊像は石川総茂が奉納した厨子に護られて無傷であった。 社殿は摂社としては破格の[[官幣社]]建築に準じて設計されており、[[神武天皇即位紀元|皇紀]]2600年とされた[[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年）に再建され、同年4月12日に正遷座祭が行われた。 戦前、楠木正成は天皇のために尽くした忠臣として大いに称えられたため、大勢の参詣者が建水分神社と南木神社に参詣している。 == 境内 == * 本殿（国[[登録有形文化財]]） - [[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年）再建。[[2004年]]（[[平成]]16年）と[[2017年]]（平成29年）に改修。一間社[[春日造]]。 * 幣殿（国登録有形文化財） * 拝殿（国登録有形文化財） - 1940年（昭和15年）建立。2004年（平成16年）に改修。本殿の前に建ち、向拝の中央間を一段高くして向唐破風屋根を付け、背面には唐破風屋根の幣殿が接続する特異な形式。 * 築地塀（国登録有形文化財） - [[1938年]]（昭和13年）築。 * 由緒標 - [[大日本帝国陸軍|陸軍]][[大将]][[荒木貞夫]][[男爵]]による筆。 Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|本殿 Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|拝殿及び幣殿 Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|楠木正成木像 == 文化財 == === 国登録有形文化財 === [[2020年]]（[[令和]]2年）7月17日、以下が[[文化庁]]から[[登録有形文化財]]に答申された{{citeweb|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642 |publisher=大阪府|title=国登録有形文化財（建造物）の登録について|accessdate=2020-11-21}}{{citeweb|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf |publisher=大阪府|title=登録文化財の詳細説明 |accessdate=2020-11-21}}。 * 本殿 * 拝殿及び幣殿 * 築地塀 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[建水分神社]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ 建水分神社] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なきしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:大阪府の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の建築物]] [[Category:日本の木造建築物]] [[Category:1940年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の歴史]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:楠木氏|社]] [[Category:楠木正成]]

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 南木神社 |画像= [[File:Nagijinjya view.jpg|260px]]
拝殿と鳥居（2020年11月） |所在地= [[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]水分357 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 36 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N |経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 37 | 経度秒 = 53 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |地図国コード = JP-29 |地図= Japan Osaka |祭神= 大楠公（[[楠木正成]]） |神体= |社格= |創建= [[延元]]2年/[[建武 (日本)|建武]]4年（[[1337年]]） |札所等= |例祭= |本殿= }} '''南木神社'''（なぎじんじゃ）は、[[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]水分にある[[神社]]。[[建水分神社]]の[[摂社]]であり、[[楠木正成]]を祀る最古の神社である。 == 歴史 == [[後醍醐天皇]]の臣・[[楠木正成]]が[[延元]]元年/[[建武 (日本)|建武]]3年（[[1336年]]）5月25日に[[湊川の戦い]]で[[足利尊氏]]に敗れ、自害した。その翌年の延元2年/建武4年（[[1337年]]）に、[[楠木氏]]の[[氏神]]である[[建水分神社]]の境内に正成を祭神とする神社を建てることとなり、[[後村上天皇]]から「楠木」の名字をもじった南木（なぎ）明神という神号を賜わり、南木神社が建水分神社の本殿の背後に建立された。 [[江戸時代]]にはこの近辺は[[伊勢国]][[神戸藩]][[石川氏]]の領地となっていた。そのため、[[元禄]]10年（[[1697年]]）に本殿が傾いた際は藩主・[[石川総茂]]の命により現在地に移転している。 [[1934年]]（[[昭和]]9年）の[[室戸台風]]により近くの老松が折れてしまい本殿は倒壊した。しかし、御神体である大楠公（楠木正成）尊像は石川総茂が奉納した厨子に護られて無傷であった。 社殿は摂社としては破格の[[官幣社]]建築に準じて設計されており、[[神武天皇即位紀元|皇紀]]2600年とされた[[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年）に再建され、同年4月12日に正遷座祭が行われた。 戦前、楠木正成は天皇のために尽くした忠臣として大いに称えられたため、大勢の参詣者が建水分神社と南木神社に参詣している。 == 境内 == * 本殿（国[[登録有形文化財]]） - [[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年）再建。[[2004年]]（[[平成]]16年）と[[2017年]]（平成29年）に改修。一間社[[春日造]]。 * 幣殿（国登録有形文化財） * 拝殿（国登録有形文化財） - 1940年（昭和15年）建立。2004年（平成16年）に改修。本殿の前に建ち、向拝の中央間を一段高くして向唐破風屋根を付け、背面には唐破風屋根の幣殿が接続する特異な形式。 * 築地塀（国登録有形文化財） - [[1938年]]（昭和13年）築。 * 由緒標 - [[大日本帝国陸軍|陸軍]][[大将]][[荒木貞夫]][[男爵]]による筆。 Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|本殿 Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|拝殿及び幣殿 Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|楠木正成木像 == 文化財 == === 国登録有形文化財 === [[2020年]]（[[令和]]2年）7月17日、以下が[[文化庁]]から[[登録有形文化財]]に答申された{{citeweb|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642 |publisher=大阪府|title=国登録有形文化財（建造物）の登録について|accessdate=2020-11-21}}{{citeweb|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf |publisher=大阪府|title=登録文化財の詳細説明 |accessdate=2020-11-21}}。 * 本殿 * 拝殿及び幣殿 * 築地塀 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[建水分神社]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ 建水分神社] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なきしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:大阪府の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の建築物]] [[Category:日本の木造建築物]] [[Category:1940年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の歴史]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:楠木氏|社]] [[Category:楠木正成]]

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}. == History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished..."
revision.text:
'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * **Main Shrine** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * **Heiden (Offerings Hall)** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * **Haiden (Worship Hall)** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * **Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * **Historical Marker** - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}}
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]
{{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chihayaakasaka]] [[Category:Japanese wooden buildings]] [[Category:1940 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Chihayaakasaka history]] [[Category:Sessha and Massha]] [[Category:Kusunoki clan]] [[Category:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ja:南木神社]]

It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * **Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * **Historical Marker** - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.

revision.text:
'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * **Main Shrine** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * **Heiden (Offerings Hall)** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * **Haiden (Worship Hall)** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * **Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * **Historical Marker** - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei

== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * **Main Shrine** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * **Heiden (Offerings Hall)** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * **Haiden (Worship Hall)** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * **Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)** (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * **Historical Marker** - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei

== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]

It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.

revision.text:
'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei

It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.

revision.text:
'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei

== Related Topics == * {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}}
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]

It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. revision.text:
'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>

During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}}
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]

'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}}
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]

revision.text:
'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>

The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.

'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei
== References == {{Reflist}}

== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]

revision.text:
'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}}
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]

It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}}
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]

In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|12=draft}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂|12=draft}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風|12=draft}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子|12=draft}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀|12=draft}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造|12=draft}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫|12=draft}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍|12=draft}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁|12=draft}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]
{{Shinto-stub}}

'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村|12=draft}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡|12=draft}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社|12=draft}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|12=draft}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|12=draft}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|12=draft}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|12=draft}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|12=draft}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元|12=draft}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|12=draft}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇|12=draft}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩|12=draft}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏|12=draft}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|12=draft}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂|12=draft}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風|12=draft}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子|12=draft}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀|12=draft}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造|12=draft}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫|12=draft}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍|12=draft}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁|12=draft}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 南木神社 |画像= [[File:Nagijinjya view.jpg|260px]]
拝殿と鳥居（2020年11月） |所在地= [[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]水分357 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 36 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N |経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 37 | 経度秒 = 53 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |地図国コード = JP-29 |地図= Japan Osaka |祭神= 大楠公（[[楠木正成]]） |神体= |社格= |創建= [[延元]]2年/[[建武 (日本)|建武]]4年（[[1337年]]） |札所等= |例祭= |本殿= }} '''南木神社'''（なぎじんじゃ）は、[[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]水分にある[[神社]]。[[建水分神社]]の[[摂社]]であり、[[楠木正成]]を祀る最古の神社である。 == 歴史 == [[後醍醐天皇]]の臣・[[楠木正成]]が[[延元]]元年/[[建武 (日本)|建武]]3年（[[1336年]]）5月25日に[[湊川の戦い]]で[[足利尊氏]]に敗れ、自害した。その翌年の延元2年/建武4年（[[1337年]]）に、[[楠木氏]]の[[氏神]]である[[建水分神社]]の境内に正成を祭神とする神社を建てることとなり、[[後村上天皇]]から「楠木」の名字をもじった南木（なぎ）明神という神号を賜わり、南木神社が建水分神社の本殿の背後に建立された。 [[江戸時代]]にはこの近辺は[[伊勢国]][[神戸藩]][[石川氏]]の領地となっていた。そのため、[[元禄]]10年（[[1697年]]）に本殿が傾いた際は藩主・[[石川総茂]]の命により現在地に移転している。 [[1934年]]（[[昭和]]9年）の[[室戸台風]]により近くの老松が折れてしまい本殿は倒壊した。しかし、御神体である大楠公（楠木正成）尊像は石川総茂が奉納した厨子に護られて無傷であった。 社殿は摂社としては破格の[[官幣社]]建築に準じて設計されており、[[神武天皇即位紀元|皇紀]]2600年とされた[[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年）に再建され、同年4月12日に正遷座祭が行われた。 戦前、楠木正成は天皇のために尽くした忠臣として大いに称えられたため、大勢の参詣者が建水分神社と南木神社に参詣している。 == 境内 == * 本殿（国[[登録有形文化財]]） - [[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年）再建。[[2004年]]（[[平成]]16年）と[[2017年]]（平成29年）に改修。一間社[[春日造]]。 * 幣殿（国登録有形文化財） * 拝殿（国登録有形文化財） - 1940年（昭和15年）建立。2004年（平成16年）に改修。本殿の前に建ち、向拝の中央間を一段高くして向唐破風屋根を付け、背面には唐破風屋根の幣殿が接続する特異な形式。 * 築地塀（国登録有形文化財） - [[1938年]]（昭和13年）築。 * 由緒標 - [[大日本帝国陸軍|陸軍]][[大将]][[荒木貞夫]][[男爵]]による筆。 Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|本殿 Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|拝殿及び幣殿 Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|楠木正成木像 == 文化財 == === 国登録有形文化財 === [[2020年]]（[[令和]]2年）7月17日、以下が[[文化庁]]から[[登録有形文化財]]に答申された{{Cite web|和書|url= https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642 |publisher=大阪府|title=国登録有形文化財（建造物）の登録について|accessdate=2020-11-21}}{{Cite web|和書|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf |publisher=大阪府|title=登録文化財の詳細説明 |accessdate=2020-11-21}}。 * 本殿 * 拝殿及び幣殿 * 築地塀 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[建水分神社]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ 建水分神社] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なきしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:大阪府の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の建築物]] [[Category:日本の木造建築物]] [[Category:1940年竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の歴史]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:楠木氏|社]] [[Category:楠木正成]]

'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡|qq=draft}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=draft}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|qq=draft}}.

The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元|qq=draft}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=draft}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇|qq=draft}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.

The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂|qq=draft}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風|qq=draft}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子|qq=draft}}.

It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造|qq=draft}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫|qq=draft}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍|qq=draft}} officer.

== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁|qq=draft}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihayaakasaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikawachi District, Osaka|qq=}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sessha|qq=}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesuimori Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masashige|qq=}}.
== History == {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Daigo|qq=}}’s vassal {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masashige|qq=}} perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga Takauji|qq=}}. The following year, in 1337 ({{ill|Engen (era)|ja|延元|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engen (era)|qq=}} 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesuimori Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=}} to enshrine Masashige. {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|ja|後村上天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Murakami|qq=}} bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the {{ill|Kambe Domain|ja|神戸藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kambe Domain|qq=}}, ruled by the {{ill|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|ja|石川氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa clan (daimyō)|qq=}}. In 1697 ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku|qq=}} 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa Sosuke|qq=}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the {{ill|Muroto typhoon|ja|室戸台風|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muroto typhoon|qq=}}. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zushi|qq=}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial reign in Japan|qq=}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga-zukuri|qq=}} style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki Sadao|qq=}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Japanese Army|qq=}} officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|ja|文化庁|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agency for Cultural Affairs|qq=}}:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesuimori Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]

It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General {{ill|Araki Sadao|ja|荒木貞夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araki Sadao|qq=}}, a baron and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|ja|大日本帝国陸軍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Japanese Army|qq=}} officer.

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
'''Nagi Shrine''' (南木神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chihayaakasaka|Chihayaakasaka]], [[:en:Minamikawachi District, Osaka|Minamikawachi District, Osaka]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sessha|qq=}} of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesuimori Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=}} and is the oldest shrine dedicated to [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]].
== History == [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]’s vassal [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]] perished in the [[Battle of Minatogawa]] on May 25, 1336, against [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. The following year, in 1337 ([[:en:Engen|Engen (era)]] 2/[[Kenmu]] 4), a shrine was constructed within the precincts of {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesuimori Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=}} to enshrine Masashige. [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] bestowed the shrine with the divine title "Nagi Myōjin," a play on the Kusunoki (楠木) surname.
During the [[Edo period]], the area became part of the domain of the [[:en:Kanbe Domain|Kambe Domain]], ruled by the [[:en:Ishikawa clan|Ishikawa clan (daimyō)]]. In 1697 ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 10), the shrine was relocated to its current location following the collapse of the original building. The move was carried out under the order of {{ill|Ishikawa Sosuke|ja|石川総茂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa Sosuke|qq=}}.
In 1934, the shrine’s main building was destroyed by the [[:en:1934 Muroto typhoon|Muroto typhoon]]. However, the enshrined statue of Masashige was undamaged, protected within a donated {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zushi|qq=}}.
The shrine's current buildings, constructed in 1940 to commemorate the 2600th year of the {{ill|Imperial reign in Japan|ja|皇紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial reign in Japan|qq=}}, follow a design inspired by [[kanpeisha]] architecture. The shrine was officially rededicated on April 12, 1940.
Before [[World War II]], Kusunoki Masashige was celebrated as a loyal retainer to the Emperor, attracting many worshippers to both Takesuimori Shrine and Nagi Shrine.
== Grounds == The shrine’s precincts feature several registered cultural properties and notable structures: * '''Main Shrine''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940 and refurbished in 2004 and 2017. It follows a single-bay [[:en:Kasuga-zukuri|Kasuga-zukuri]] style. * '''Heiden (Offerings Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden (Worship Hall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940 and renovated in 2004. It is notable for its unique design, featuring a karahafu roof at the front central section and connecting to the heiden at the rear. * '''Tsuijibei (Earthen Wall)''' (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1938. * '''Historical Marker''' - Inscribed by General [[:en:Sadao Araki|Araki Sadao]], a baron and [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] officer.
<gallery> File:Nagijinjya Hondo.jpg|Main Shrine File:Nagijinjya Haiden.jpg|Worship and Offerings Halls File:Statue of Kusunoki Masashige at Nagi shrine.jpg|Wooden statue of Kusunoki Masashige </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === On July 17, 2020, the following were designated as Registered Tangible Cultural Properties by the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]]:<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028024021/http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/index.php?site=fumin&pageId=38642|title=Regarding the Registration of National Tangible Cultural Properties (Buildings)|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/hodo/attach/hodo-38642_5.pdf|title=Details on Registered Cultural Properties|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2020-11-21}}</ref> * Main Shrine * Worship Hall and Offerings Hall * Tsuijibei
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesuimori Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Take Mikumari Shrine|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Takesuimori Shrine Website]
